1.title: Neurology Note
description: Chief Complaint: Resolving encephalopathy with right hemiparesis

HPI: 65 Yo female with hx of lung adenocarcinoma with no metastases to bone, right adrenal gland, multiple lymph nodes, PE on Xarelto, HTN presenting with combination of encephalopathy and resolving right hemiparesis and sensory loss.

Around 5 PM on 9/15/2025, patient was at a birthday dinner and was experiencing headache, blurry and perhaps double vision with dizziness.  Around 8:30 PM she was off balance/having difficulty walking but did not drink any alcohol.  Around 10 PM, she was noted to be unresponsive, blank staring, sitting with no witnessed seizure activity but became responsive after 1-2 minutes with observed right facial weakness and right-sided limb weakness with reportedly flaccid right arm initially. She was brought to the hospital for evaluation.  Initial NIHSS was 4 with points from somnolence/inattention (encephalopathy) with disorientation, right arm drift and sensory loss.  Not a candidate for thrombolytics due to suspected last normal time and use of Xarelto.  Qualitatively, there was concern the patient appeared to be postictal.

Her lung adenocarcinoma initially came to medical attention on chest x-ray from 6/25/2025 due to chest pain.  Sputum cytology from the same day showed malignant non-small cell lung cancer cells.  Right supraclavicular lymph node biopsy from 7/2/2025 showed metastatic pulmonary adenocarcinoma.  Body PET/CT from 7/6/2025 showed a large hypermetabolic left lower lobe lung mass consistent with known malignancy, malignant left pleural effusion, extensive hypermetabolic lymphadenopathy of the neck and chest consistent with metastases, right adrenal metastasis, and "extensive" osseous metastasis that on my personal review involves the majority of the pelvis, femoral heads, humeral heads, multiple vertebrae, and uncertain involvement of the calvarium.

MRI brain from 7/11/2025 showed no brain parenchymal abnormalities but several small areas of enhancement seen throughout the calvarium as well as some bone marrow signal abnormality involving the visualized C-spine concerning for osseous metastases.

As of 7/11/2025, patient started on carboplatin and pemetrexed and added zoledronic acid.  She underwent radiation therapy to painful bone metastases.  Started on osimertinib (Tagrisso) due to EGFR positive mutation.
Patient is not on aspirin, statin, or antiepileptics at baseline.
9/16/25: Currently, patient is poorly communicative with right sided weakness and concern for decreased perception on the right.  Otherwise, patient is unable to provide meaningful history.

MRI of the brain with and without contrast was obtained and was significantly motion degraded and also limited by artifact from braces.  However, within these limitations there are multifocal bilateral areas of acute ischemic stroke most prominently of the right parietal lobe, left posterior frontal/parietal lobe and scattered throughout the bilateral occipital and temporal lobes concerning for acute ischemic stroke.  She has areas consistent with remote encephalomalacia of the bilateral cerebellum.  Although there does not appear to be clear hemorrhagic transformation of her areas of ischemic stroke, there are numerous punctate well-circumscribed areas of susceptibility artifact within the bilateral cerebral cortex or potentially in the subarachnoid space.  These findings might be consistent with remote hemorrhages but are concerning for hypervascular masses/possible metastases (formal radiology review is pending).  Note the postcontrast sequences are severely motion degraded and essentially uninterpretable.

9/17/25: CT angiogram of the head/neck was obtained and shows irregular filling of the distal cervical left ICA with impaired filling and possible intraluminal thrombus within the petrous portion of the left ICA with eventual nonopacification and complete vessel occlusion in the cavernous and supraclinoid segments.  There appears to be good filling of the left MCA and ACA likely via a robust ACOM and other collaterals.  There is mostly fetal origin of the bilateral posterior communicating arteries but the left PCA has modest connection to the basilar artery and appears to be filling normally.
Clinically, there are no significant changes

9/18/25: Noncontrast CT scan of the head was obtained yesterday and showed interval development of cytotoxic edema primarily within the right parietal lobe but also scattered areas within the left cerebral hemisphere.  There were a couple areas of hyperdensity within the subarachnoid space including left frontal lobe and left paramedian parietal lobe possibly representing acute blood products, but uncertain.  Differential diagnosis including residual contrast extra-axial vascularized masses or thrombus.
Repeat noncontrast CT scan of the head showed no significant change in the evolving cytotoxic edema/hypodensity within the bilateral cerebral hemispheres as well as the subarachnoid hyperdensities.
There is reportedly a change in her alertness and interactivity yesterday, becoming poorly responsive.
Today, she remains more somnolent and poorly communicative.
9/19/25: EEG yesterday showed significant diffuse slowing without epileptiform discharges.  Patient remains somnolent and poorly interactive.  Otherwise, no clear neurologic changes

65 Yo female with hx of lung adenocarcinoma with no metastases to bone, right adrenal gland, multiple lymph nodes, PE on Xarelto, HTN presenting with combination of encephalopathy and resolving right hemiparesis and sensory loss.

Patient presented with an episode of unresponsiveness followed by clear relatively diffuse left hemispheric dysfunction.  Patient was found to have extensive bilateral acute ischemic strokes thought to be due to hypercoagulability of malignancy despite therapeutic anticoagulation on Xarelto.  CT angiogram shows occlusion of the left intracranial ICA with good filling of the left MCA and ACA.

Note that patient's MRI of the head from 7/11/2025 did not show any evidence of brain parenchymal abnormalities or meningeal lesions, but patient has known osseous metastases to the calvarium.  Patient has numerous superficial areas of parenchymal susceptibility artifact that are potentially concerning for hyper vascularized micrometastases, but postcontrast sequences on recent MRI were nondiagnostic

My primary clinical concern is that patient has a high risk of recurrent ischemic strokes especially given that she experienced a multifocal bilateral ischemic stroke while therapeutically anticoagulated on rivaroxaban.  Although there is theoretically risk of hemorrhagic transformation with therapeutic anticoagulation, a recent study published in the Lancet 2025 (CATALYST) supports that early initiation of therapeutic anticoagulation after ischemic stroke is likely favorable with significantly decreased ischemic events and no significant increase in symptomatic hemorrhage.
-Continue therapeutic Lovenox while patient is unable to take p.o.
- Recommend repeat MRI of the brain with and without contrast to evaluate for intracranial metastases or any increase in ischemia burden
- STAT CT head for any acute changes in neurologic exam
- Apply SCDs, pharmacologic DVT prophylaxis is neurologically appropriate

Patient sleepy and inattentive 9/18/2025 of yet undetermined etiology.  I do not see clear evidence of new focal neurologic deficits, and her noncontrast CT scans of the head showed expected interval findings compared with prior MRI of the brain.  There are a couple of extra-axial hyperdensities that might be consistent with small subarachnoid hemorrhage/blood products, but these findings may also represent hyper vascularized masses such as was considered on her motion-degraded MRI.  These hypodensities are not changing and do not appear to represent a contraindication to ongoing therapeutic anticoagulation.

Clinically, she appears to have global cerebral dysfunction without clear explanatory factors.  So far, no evidence of new diffuse ischemia or focal brainstem/thalamic lesions, and no severe progressive cytotoxic edema. Seizures are potentially on the differential diagnosis, but patient without any clear clinical seizure activity and initial EEG negative.  However, patient remains severely encephalopathic without alternative etiology, will consider empirically treating for seizures given high clinical suspicion.

- No clear indication for antiepileptics currently but will consider empiric antiepileptics if patient remains severely somnolent without alternative explanation given the possibility for intermittent subclinical electrographic seizures that may be missed on EEG monitoring
- Recommend repeat MRI of the brain with and without contrast as above
- Promote sleep hygiene with appropriate day/night divisions (e.g. lights on during day and off during night, turning off the TV after 10 PM, opening the blinds in the morning)
- Ensure patient has access to any needed sensory aids (e.g. glasses, hearing aids)

- Avoid or limit deliriogenic medications such as: Anticholinergics, Benzodiazepines (including zolpidem, zaleplon, and eszopiclone), opiates, antihistamine, muscle relaxants, dopamine agonists, MAO inhibitors, steroids, certain antibiotics (cefepime, metronidazole, fluoroquinolones, linezolid), digitalis, lithium, calcineurin inhibitors
